meta.name: Unstable Bradycardia — Complete Heart Block, Atropine-Refractory, Transcutaneous Pacing

patient.name: Walter Borowski
patient.chiefComplaint: Syncope while walking from his recliner to the bathroom about 20 minutes ago; came to on the floor confused, now lightheaded, weak, with a 'really slow' pulse felt by his wife.

history.hpi: 82-year-old retired electrician was watching the evening news in his recliner. He stood up to go to the bathroom and made it about three steps before his wife saw him 'go gray' and crumple to the carpet. He was unresponsive for approximately 20–30 seconds, did not have any seizure-like activity, and did not strike his head (rug-covered floor; she heard the fall but the recliner blocked a hard impact). When he came around he was confused for a few minutes, then oriented but exhausted and pale. His wife checked his radial pulse and found it 'really slow.' He has felt 'off' for two days — fatigue, exertional shortness of breath while taking the trash out, and one earlier 'gray-out' that he didn't tell anyone about. Denies chest pain, denies palpitations. He has CAD post-CABG ×3 (15 years ago), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease (stage 3). Of note, his cardiologist increased his metoprolol succinate from 25 mg to 50 mg about three weeks ago for atrial ectopy. He took his usual evening dose tonight.
pastMedical: Mild dementia (per wife — slowed memory, no formal diagnosis)
medications: Metoprolol succinate 50 mg PO daily (increased from 25 mg three weeks ago)
history.lastOralIntake: Dinner about 90 minutes ago — chicken, rice, green beans; coffee earlier
history.events: Witnessed syncope while standing up from recliner ~20 minutes prior to EMS arrival; ~20–30 seconds of unresponsiveness; no head strike. Two days of progressive fatigue, one prior unwitnessed 'gray-out.' Recent metoprolol dose increase three weeks ago.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 82-year-old male, syncopal episode at home, now conscious but pale and weak. Wife on scene at single-family residence. ALS response.
scene.location: Living room of a single-story ranch house. Patient supine on the carpet beside the recliner; coffee table moved aside. Front door open with adequate stretcher access via the foyer.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Wife cooperative and articulate. Small terrier dog secured in the bedroom by an adult son who arrived with EMS.
scene.bystanders: Wife (primary historian) and adult son (just arrived). Wife states he was on the news in the recliner, stood up to go to the bathroom, and collapsed. She heard the fall but he did not hit anything hard. He was 'out cold' for under a minute. His pulse felt 'really slow.' She brought his pill bottles in a bag.
visualCues: Elderly man supine on living room carpet, pale and gray-tinged
Tongue color appears normal; no oral trauma
Pill bottles bagged on the coffee table — metoprolol prominently visible
Wife holding a written list of medications and doctors
TV still on with evening news; recliner reclined behind patient
No vomit, no incontinence, no signs of trauma

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds an 82-year-old man on the carpet, alert but pale and clearly ill. Initial monitor connection shows third-degree AV block — atrial rate ~85 with completely independent ventricular escape rate of 28. BP is 78/42 with cool extremities and mild confusion. The clinical picture is unstable bradycardia from complete heart block, likely worsened by his recent metoprolol dose escalation on top of age-related conduction disease. Atropine will be tried per protocol but is unlikely to work in CHB because the block is below the AV node — the teaching point is to PREPARE TCP at the same time atropine is being drawn, not after it has failed.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 18, mildly labored, lungs with bibasilar crackles (chronic baseline per wife)
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Slow regular pulse at radial — 28 bpm; cool, pale, mottled extremities; cap refill 4 sec
otherFindings: Monitor: third-degree AV block — atrial rate ~85, regular wide-QRS escape at 28
SBP 78 — hypotensive
Mild confusion — cannot recall day or sequence of events
No chest pain, no obvious focal neuro deficit
Recent metoprolol dose increase three weeks ago — possible contributor
Unstable bradycardia → atropine + prepare TCP simultaneously
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (slow, soft) I just got up to go to the bathroom and the next thing I know I was on the floor. My heart feels like it's barely going. I'm lightheaded but I'm here.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey; identify instability — AMS + hypotension + CHB = UNSTABLE bradycardia
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Inclusion Criteria — HR <60 with AMS, syncope, shock, or hemodynamic instability defines treatment indication.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Inclusion Criteria: Heart rate less than 60 beats per minute (BPM) with either symptoms (altered mental status (AMS), chest pain (CP), congestive heart failure (CHF), seizure, syncope, shock, pallor, diaphoresis) or evidence of hemodynamic instability.

expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Adult Management 1–2 — manage airway, oxygen targeted 94–98%.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult Management: Manage airway as necessary. Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor; pacer pads on (TCP pre-armed); 12-lead EKG
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia step 3 — initiate monitoring and 12-lead. Pre-arming TCP minimizes time to capture if/when atropine fails.

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access ×2 large-bore (atropine, sedation, push-dose epi)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia step 4 — establish IV access; multiple lines support escalating therapy.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia driver
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia step 5 — check glucose; treat hypoglycemia if present.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia per the Hypoglycemia Guideline and Hyperglycemia Guideline.

expectedActions.shortAction: Atropine 1 mg IV; recognize CHB unlikely to respond → prepare TCP simultaneously
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Adult Management 6 + Educational Pearl 6 — atropine 1 mg q3–5 min max 3 mg; third-degree block may not respond → proceed quickly to TCP.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Atropine 1 mg IV q 3–5 minutes (maximum total dose of 3 mg). Third-degree heart block or the denervated heart (as in cardiac transplant) may not respond to atropine and in these cases, proceed quickly to chronotropic agents (such as epinephrine or dopamine) or transcutaneous pacing. In cases of impending hemodynamic collapse, proceed directly to transcutaneous pacing.

transitions.if: Atropine ineffective; TCP started with sedation/analgesia
transitions.condition: Atropine 1 mg IV given without effect (escape rate unchanged, BP unchanged); crew immediately escalates to transcutaneous pacing with concurrent sedation/analgesia (fentanyl + small midazolam) and titrates pacer for capture.
Crew goes straight to TCP without giving any sedation or analgesia; the alert patient experiences each pacing impulse as severe pain, becomes agitated, and loses cooperation.

phases.name: Atropine ineffective; TCP with sedation
phases.description: Atropine 1 mg IV given — no change in rate or rhythm, as expected for infranodal block. Crew explains TCP to the patient and wife. Sedation/analgesia given before pacing: fentanyl 80 mcg IV (80 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 80 mcg, max 100 mcg) plus midazolam 1 mg IV slow push (small initial increment given age 82, hypotension, and elderly dose reduction). Pacer started at 70 bpm and the output (mA) titrated upward; mechanical capture confirmed at 70 mA with palpable femoral and radial pulses corresponding 1:1 to pacer spikes. BP rises to 102/64 and mentation clears within 30–60 seconds.
phases.triggerCondition: Atropine 1 mg IV given without effect; crew escalates to TCP at 70 bpm with concurrent fentanyl + low-dose midazolam; pacer current titrated up until mechanical capture is confirmed.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 16, lungs with chronic crackles unchanged
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong pulses at carotid, femoral, AND radial corresponding 1:1 to pacer spikes; warm skin returning
otherFindings: Atropine 1 mg IV given — no effect (expected for CHB)
Pre-pacing fentanyl 80 mcg + midazolam 1 mg given
TCP started at 70 bpm; output titrated up
Mechanical capture confirmed at 70 mA — palpable pulse 1:1 with pacer spike
BP improved to 102/64
Mentation cleared — patient now oriented ×3
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (more alert) That medicine helped right before the pacing started. I can feel a thump but it's tolerable. Thank you. I feel my head clearing up.

expectedActions.shortAction: Fentanyl 80 mcg IV (80 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) — analgesia before pacing
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 6b — fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV/IM/IN/IO max 100 mcg; pre-pacing analgesia per Bradycardia Patient Safety #1.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For Moderate to Severe pain, analgesics include: Fentanyl: 1 mcg/kg IN, IM, IV or IO (maximum initial dose of 100 mcg).

expectedActions.shortAction: Midazolam 1 mg slow IV — anxiolysis (reduced increment for age 82, hypotension)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Patient Safety + Pain Management Patient Safety — sedation/pain control with pacing; reduce dose in elderly.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: If pacing is performed, consider sedation or pain control.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transcutaneous pacing at 70 bpm; titrate mA up; confirm electrical AND mechanical capture
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Adult Management 6 + Educational Pearl — TCP for atropine-refractory or hemodynamic collapse; capture confirmation includes palpable pulse 1:1 with spikes.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Transcutaneous Pacing – If pacing is performed, consider sedation or pain control. Bradycardia should be managed via the least invasive manner possible, escalating care as needed. Third-degree heart block or the denervated heart (as in cardiac transplant) may not respond to atropine and in these cases, proceed quickly to chronotropic agents (such as epinephrine or dopamine) or transcutaneous pacing. In cases of impending hemodynamic collapse, proceed directly to transcutaneous pacing.

expectedActions.shortAction: Allergies, BP, SpO2, capnography on; reassess pain/RR every 5 min during pacing
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 7 + Patient Safety #1, #2 — capnography earlier predictor of hypoventilation; caution with opioids in hypotension/elderly; reassess.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use of non-invasive capnography is an earlier predictor of hypoventilation than pulse oximetry if opioid medications are administered. Patient Safety Considerations: Administer opioids with caution to patients with Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) less than 15, hypotension, identified medication allergy, hypoxia (SPO2 less than 90%) after maximal supplemental oxygen therapy, or signs of hypoventilation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV ready (1 mL of 10 mcg/mL dilution) if BP fails to recover with capture
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Vasopressor 2 — push-dose epinephrine 10–20 mcg q2 min, titrated to MAP >65, as an alternative/adjunct to TCP.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Vasopressor medications: Epinephrine by push dose (dilute boluses): for example, prepare 10 mcg/mL by adding 1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL epinephrine to 9 mL of normal saline, then administer 10–20 mcg boluses (1–2 mL) q 2 minutes titrated MAP greater than 65 mmHg.

transitions.if: Capture confirmed; transport to PCI/EP-capable facility
transitions.condition: Mechanical capture confirmed; BP and mentation improved on pacing; patient transported with continuous TCP, sedation as needed, monitoring pad-site burns, and reassessment.

phases.name: Capture confirmed; transport on TCP
phases.description: Patient transported with continuous transcutaneous pacing to a PCI- and electrophysiology-capable receiving hospital. Mechanical capture continuously confirmed by palpation of femoral pulse synchronous with pacer spikes. Sedation/analgesia titrated as needed for pacing discomfort during transport. Pad sites monitored. Bedside handoff includes initial rhythm, atropine attempt, joules-equivalent (mA), capture threshold, sedation given, and recent metoprolol dose increase as a likely contributor.
phases.triggerCondition: Mechanical capture confirmed; hemodynamics and mentation improved on TCP; transport with continuous pacing and reassessment.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong pulses 1:1 with pacer; warm and dry
otherFindings: Continuous capture maintained on TCP at 70 bpm
Pad sites checked — no skin breakdown
Mild residual sedation; patient relaxed and oriented
Wife following separately to hospital
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I'm tolerating the pacing. The thumping is steady. I feel a lot more like myself.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess capture continuously: pulse 1:1 with spikes; titrate mA if loses capture
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Documentation — pacing time, rate, joules, capture, and response are required documentation elements.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Cardiac rhythm/rate. Time, dose, and response of medications given. Pacing: Time started or stopped, rate, joules, capture, and response rate.

expectedActions.shortAction: Titrate sedation/analgesia for pacing comfort; reassess RR + capnography q5 min
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 9 — repeat dosing if vital signs allow; capnography for hypoventilation surveillance.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If indicated based on pain assessment, and vital signs allow, repeat pain medication administration (excluding acetaminophen and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines) after 5 minutes of the previous dose. Use of non-invasive capnography is an earlier predictor of hypoventilation than pulse oximetry if opioid medications are administered.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to PCI/EP-capable hospital; bedside handoff with rhythm strips + dose timeline
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Documentation — full handoff including rhythm changes, pacing details, and history supporting underlying cause (recent metoprolol dose escalation).
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Cardiac rhythm/rate. Pacing: Time started or stopped, rate, joules, capture, and response rate. Patient weight. History of event supporting treatment of underlying causes.

phases.name: TCP started without sedation
phases.description: Crew started TCP without giving any pre-pacing sedation or analgesia. Capture is achieved electrically and mechanically, BP improves — but the alert patient experiences each pacing impulse as a severe muscular jolt and shouts in pain. He becomes agitated, tries to remove the pads, and his wife is distressed at the bedside. The protocol explicitly states 'If pacing is performed, consider sedation or pain control' — this is not optional comfort care but a safety/efficacy issue, because an agitated patient who fights the pacer can lose capture and pull off the pads. The crew now must give analgesia and anxiolysis post-hoc and de-escalate while maintaining capture.
phases.triggerCondition: TCP started without pre-pacing sedation/analgesia in an alert patient; capture achieved but patient agitated and at risk of dislodging pads.

skin.color: flushed with anxiety, prior pallor improving

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Reaching for the pads, pushing crew hands away
otherFindings: Capture confirmed mechanically — clinically the right outcome
Patient agitated, in distress
Wife very upset at bedside
Risk of pad displacement and lost capture
Lesson moment: sedation BEFORE pacing
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Stop! That hurts! Why didn't you give me anything? Get this off me!

expectedActions.shortAction: Fentanyl 80 mcg IV NOW (80 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) for pacing pain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 6b — fentanyl 1 mcg/kg max 100 mcg; reactive analgesia for pacing-induced pain.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For Moderate to Severe pain, analgesics include: Fentanyl: 1 mcg/kg IN, IM, IV or IO (maximum initial dose of 100 mcg). If indicated based on pain assessment, and vital signs allow, repeat pain medication administration (excluding acetaminophen and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines) after 5 minutes of the previous dose.

expectedActions.shortAction: Midazolam 1 mg slow IV — anxiolysis (reduced increment for age 82)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Patient Safety — sedation/pain control with pacing; reduce dose in elderly given hypotension and age.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: If pacing is performed, consider sedation or pain control.

expectedActions.shortAction: Verbal reassurance; explain procedure to patient and wife; non-pharmacologic measures
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 4 — non-pharmacologic options including verbal reassurance to control anxiety.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-pharmacologic pain management options include: Placement of the patient in a position of comfort. Application of ice packs and/or splints for pain secondary to trauma. Verbal reassurance to control anxiety.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recheck pad adhesion; confirm continued capture; transport without losing pacing
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Documentation — pacing rate, capture, and response are required; ensuring continued capture is the immediate priority.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Pacing: Time started or stopped, rate, joules, capture, and response rate.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document plan: pre-pacing sedation/analgesia in future TCP; QI debrief
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Bradycardia Performance Measures — use of sedation/pain management with pacing is explicitly tracked.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Blood sugar obtained. Correct medication(s) and dose given for patient condition, age, and weight. Correct application and use of cardiac pacing. Use of sedation or pain management with cardiac pacing.
